# ConsentGraph

> Consent Graph as Code: deterministic action governance for AI agents.

## What it does

ConsentGraph lets you define exactly what an AI agent is authorized to do — in a single JSON file with a simple Python API. Every action is classified into one of four tiers:

- SILENT: execute autonomously, log only
- VISIBLE: execute and notify the operator afterward
- FORCED: require explicit operator approval before executing
- BLOCKED: never execute under any circumstances, alert operator

## Installation

```
pip install consentgraph
```

## Core API

```python
from consentgraph import check_consent, log_override, ConsentGraphConfig

# Configure
config = ConsentGraphConfig(graph_path="./consent-graph.json")

# Check if an action is permitted
tier = check_consent("email", "send", confidence=0.9, config=config)
# Returns: "SILENT" | "VISIBLE" | "FORCED" | "BLOCKED"

# Log when operator overrides a decision
log_override("email", "send", reason="user confirmed", operator_decision="approved", config=config)
```

## CLI

```bash
consentgraph init              # create example consent-graph.json
consentgraph check email send  # check a single action
consentgraph summary           # human-readable policy overview
consentgraph validate          # validate graph against schema
consentgraph mcp               # start MCP server (stdio)
```

## MCP Integration

ConsentGraph exposes a single MCP tool: `check_consent(domain, action, confidence)`.
Start the MCP server: `consentgraph mcp`
The tool returns the tier string and logs the evaluation to the audit trail.

## Consent Graph JSON Schema (v0.2)

```json
{
  "metadata": {
    "version": "0.2.0",
    "description": "...",
    "owner": "agent-name",
    "compliance_profile": "FedRAMP-High | CMMC-L3 | SOC2-Type2 | IL4 | commercial",
    "agent_id": "my-agent"
  },
  "boundary": {
    "allowed_endpoints": ["*.azure.us", "*.microsoftonline.us"],
    "deny_cross_boundary": true
  },
  "domains": {
    "<domain_name>": {
      "autonomous": ["action1", "action2"],
      "requires_approval": ["action3"],
      "blocked": ["action4"],
      "trust_level": "critical | high | medium | low",
      "control_mappings": ["AC-6", "CM-7"],
      "_design_note": "Rationale for trust tier."
    }
  },
  "delegation": {
    "trusted_agents": [
      {
        "agent_id": "deploy-agent",
        "graph_path": "./deploy-agent.json",
        "domains_allowed": ["ecs", "ecr"],
        "requires_approval": true
      }
    ],
    "max_delegation_depth": 1
  },
  "consent_decay": {
    "enabled": true,
    "review_interval_days": 30
  }
}
```

## Example Consent Graphs

Real-world reference examples in `examples/`:

| File | Use Case | Compliance |
|------|----------|------------|
| `consent-graph.example.json` | Personal assistant agent | — |
| `aws-ecs-deployment-agent.json` | AWS ECS deploy pipeline | commercial |
| `aws-cost-optimizer-agent.json` | AWS cost management agent | commercial |
| `k8s-ops-agent.json` | Kubernetes ops agent | commercial |
| `gcc-azure-gov-agent.json` | Azure Government cloud agent | FedRAMP-High / IL4 |
| `cmmc-l3-devops-agent.json` | Defense contractor DevOps agent | CMMC-L3 |
| `multi-agent-orchestrator.json` | Orchestrator delegating to sub-agents | commercial |

## Multi-Agent Delegation

An orchestrator agent's consent graph defines what it can delegate to sub-agents:

```json
"delegation": {
  "trusted_agents": [
    { "agent_id": "cost-agent", "domains_allowed": ["ec2_rightsizing"], "requires_approval": false },
    { "agent_id": "deploy-agent", "domains_allowed": ["ecs", "ecr"], "requires_approval": true }
  ],
  "max_delegation_depth": 1
}
```

This enforces AC-5 (Separation of Duties) at the policy layer: no single agent spans all domains.

## Compliance Profiles

Set `compliance_profile` in metadata to signal which control framework informed the graph.
Add `control_mappings` per domain to link actions to NIST 800-53 or 800-171 control families.
These fields are metadata only -- enforcement is still the 4-tier model.

Common profiles: `FedRAMP-High`, `FedRAMP-Moderate`, `CMMC-L3`, `CMMC-L2`, `SOC2-Type2`, `IL4`, `IL5`, `commercial`

## Audit Trail

Every consent evaluation is appended to `~/.consentgraph/logs/consent-attempts.jsonl`.
Format: `{timestamp, domain, action, confidence, tier, reason}`

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/mmartoccia/consentgraph
- PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/consentgraph/
- Schema: https://consentgraph.dev/schema.json
- Docs: https://consentgraph.dev
